ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for mazesoba; mapped to broader noodle dish concept FOODON_03315595.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for mazesoba; A0EJW (Noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for mazesoba or Japanese brothless noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Original Mazesoba weighs approximately 550 g and represents a complete single-serve meal portion as typically served at Japanese noodle restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Noodles, wheat, dry — Singapore HPB Foodo Database; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked wheat noodles (FDC ID 20110) for composite dish estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) sourced from HPB Foodo database entry for wheat noodles / mixed noodle dish. Sugar, saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C values are low-confidence estimates from delivery platform metadata and are retained with confidence 0.7 pending verification against an approved source. The input record's food_subgroup 'Baking ingredients' has been corrected to 'Noodle dishes' as mazesoba is a prepared noodle dish. Sodium value of 1 mg/100g is noted as anomalously low and likely reflects the dry noodle component only; actual dish sodium will be substantially higher once seasoning sauce is included.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat noodles (boiled)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mazesoba as a composite dish. GI of 55 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles (GI range 47–58, median ~55), which constitute the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (55 × (71.5 × 0.9 × 5.5)) / 100 ≈ (55 × 353.9) / 100 ≈ 194.6; rounded to 216.2 using full carbohydrate mass of 71.5 g/100g × 550 g serving = 393.25 g carbohydrate × 55 / 100 = 216.3 GL units.

hypertension.key_factor: Anomalously low reported sodium (1 mg/100g) reflects dry noodle base only; actual dish sodium from soy sauce tare and seasoning is substantially higher and poses a meaningful hypertension risk
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce seasoning and omit or reduce chashu pork belly, as the actual dish sodium is expected to exceed 400 mg/100g once all condiments are included; the high potassium content (460 mg/100g) and dietary fibre (10 g/100g) from the noodle base are beneficial for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High total carbohydrate (71.5 g/100g) with a large 550 g serving delivers approximately 393 g of carbohydrate per meal, producing a very high glycemic load of approximately 216 GL units
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to half a bowl or fewer, as the full 550 g serving delivers an extremely high glycemic load; the high dietary fibre (10 g/100g) partially attenuates the glycemic response but is insufficient to offset the total carbohydrate burden at standard serving size.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 3.27 g/100g and trans fat at 0.09 g/100g are present; chashu pork belly contributes additional saturated fat and sodium beyond the reported noodle-base values
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request omission or substitution of chashu pork belly with a leaner protein, reduce soy sauce seasoning to limit sodium intake, and note that the noodle base itself has a relatively low fat profile (3.4 g/100g total fat) which is acceptable within a heart-healthy diet when portion size is controlled.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Chashu pork belly (red meat) contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies inflammatory pathways; fish powder (bonito/sardine) provides anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request substitution of chashu pork belly with chicken chashu or tofu to reduce arachidonic acid load, and confirm freshness of raw egg yolk to avoid infection risk in immunocompromised patients on biologics.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Original Mazesoba is a Japanese brothless noodle dish served in a bowl without soup. Unlike traditional ramen, mazesoba (literally 'mixed noodles') is prepared by tossing thick wheat noodles in a rich, umami-forward sauce made from soy sauce tare, sesame oil, fish powder (typically bonito or sardine), and garlic. The dish is typically topped with chashu pork belly, a raw egg yolk, nori (dried seaweed), green onions, and additional condiments. The noodles are mixed vigorously at the table before eating, coating each strand in the concentrated sauce. The texture is chewy and satisfying, with a deep savoury flavour profile balanced by the richness of the egg yolk and the smokiness of the fish powder. Originating in Japan and popularised in Singapore's Japanese restaurant scene, mazesoba is served as a lunch or dinner meal. Nutritionally, the dish is carbohydrate-dominant (71.5 g/100g) with moderate protein (13.2 g/100g) and low fat (3.4 g/100g) in the noodle base, though actual dish fat and sodium increase substantially with toppings and seasoning sauce. Dietary fibre is notably high at 10 g/100g. The reported sodium of 1 mg/100g reflects the dry noodle component only and will be much higher in the assembled dish. Clinically, the very high glycemic load per serving makes this dish a high-concern food for type 2 diabetes management, while the actual sodium from seasoning raises moderate concern for hypertension. The dish is not suitable for gluten-free or halal diets in standard preparation.
common_user_queries: Is mazesoba the same as dry ramen?
How many calories in a bowl of mazesoba?
What is the difference between mazesoba and ramen?
How much carbohydrate is in mazesoba?

confidence_reason: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, dietary fibre, sodium, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) are sourced from HPB Foodo database with confidence 0.95. Secondary nutrients (sugar, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) are sourced from delivery platform metadata with confidence 0.7 and are not from an approved primary source; these values are retained as best available estimates pending verification. The sodium value of 1 mg/100g is anomalously low and likely reflects the dry noodle component only, not the assembled dish. Confidence is therefore medium overall.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this mazesoba preparation is not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the HPB Foodo source entry for this dish and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for an equivalent mazesoba composite as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per HPB source for the noodle base component; note that chashu pork belly and egg yolk toppings will contribute cholesterol to the assembled dish. 4. Sodium of 1 mg/100g is anomalously low and reflects the dry noodle base only; actual assembled dish sodium from soy sauce tare and seasoning is expected to be substantially higher (estimated 400–800 mg/100g range). 5. Sugar (1.45 g/100g), saturated fat (3.27 g/100g), trans fat (0.09 g/100g), and vitamin C (0.91 mg/100g) are sourced from delivery platform metadata (confidence 0.7) and should be verified against an approved primary source. 6. Input food_subgroup 'Baking ingredients' corrected to 'Noodle dishes' as this is a prepared noodle dish. 7. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on boiled wheat noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for mazesoba exists in approved sources.
